posts.title: Making a WebView terminal feel native
posts.feature: Performance & usability (v1.5) — near-native terminal latency study
posts.summary: A cargo build shouldn't make your terminal stutter. The engineering study behind v1.5's near-native input latency: off-main-thread rendering, a binary frame protocol, and the Apple Silicon QoS discovery that closed the gap — plus the usability sharpening that shipped alongside.

posts.title: AI Chat: an agent that reads your terminal, not its screenshot
posts.summary: Most chat sidebars are blind to your terminal. AI Chat reads structured OSC 133 state — commands, outputs, exit codes, links — runs local (Ollama) or cloud models, and can arm approval-gated reactive watchers. In beta behind the Experimental toggle.

posts.title: Command blocks for agent terminals
posts.summary: An AI agent's terminal is a wall of text. We segmented it into navigable command blocks — with detection that works even for agents that emit no markers, via heuristic ⏺ tool-call recognition alongside OSC 133 / OSC 7770.

posts.title: Auto-standby: SIGSTOP for idle agent terminals
posts.summary: Dozens of idle agent terminals quietly burn CPU and battery. Auto-standby SIGSTOPs the whole process group when a tab is unfocused and idle, and SIGCONTs it the instant you look back or a message arrives.

posts.title: A built-in process monitor for your terminal trees
posts.summary: When one of a dozen agents pins a core, which one is it? A process monitor reports CPU and RSS for TUIC and every child process tree — over MCP, over HTTP, and as a self-contained dashboard.

posts.title: SSH tunnels, remote access, and the beta features you can try today
posts.feature: SSH tunnels / tuicommander-remote / AI Triage / AI Watchers / AI Chat / beta
posts.summary: A tour of the features behind the Experimental toggle: SSH tunnel management, the tuicommander-remote daemon, AI-powered code triage, terminal watchers, and chat. All in beta — feedback welcome.

posts.title: One daemon, every environment: meet tuicommander-remote
posts.feature: tuicommander-remote / headless daemon / WSL / Docker / remote servers
posts.summary: A headless Rust daemon that runs inside WSL, Docker dev containers, and remote servers. The desktop app drives them as if they were local — same protocol, no path translation, no cross-OS shims.

posts.title: AI Triage: code review that understands what changed
posts.summary: Every AI IDE can generate code. None of them review the diff before you commit. AI Triage classifies every changed file by risk, relevance, and category — streaming results as the LLM works through your changes.

posts.title: Using Codex to orchestrate Claude Code through TUICommander
posts.feature: Cross-agent orchestration / MCP bridge / drive_agent tool
posts.summary: Cross-agent orchestration without screen-scraping. TUICommander's MCP bridge gives external agents structured terminal state instead of raw ANSI bytes. Here's how to set it up and why it beats tmux.

posts.title: Why we replaced xterm.js with a native terminal
posts.summary: After months of patching WebGL atlas corruption, scroll jump races, and scrollbar visibility hacks, we replaced xterm.js with an Alacritty-based terminal rendered to Canvas. Fewer workaround files than we had bug fixes.

posts.title: Launched on Product Hunt — and what real users say
posts.summary: TUICommander is live on Product Hunt this week. Early reviews from daily users name the same patterns: multi-agent terminal sprawl, worktree isolation, blocking Y/n prompts, and couch-mode supervision from mobile. If you use TUIC, your review moves the needle.

posts.title: What one person can ship in a month
posts.summary: Fifteen releases, 226 changelog entries, nearly 90,000 lines of code — shipped solo in evenings and weekends. A retrospective on what Claude Code actually compresses, and what it can't.

posts.title: Global Workspace: one view for all your agents
posts.summary: When you run agents across multiple repos, you lose track of who's doing what. Global Workspace pulls the terminals that matter into a single split-pane view — without moving them.

posts.title: Read, diff, and edit without leaving the terminal
posts.feature: Markdown viewer / Code editor / Side-by-side diff / Clickable paths
posts.summary: AI agents reference files constantly. Instead of alt-tabbing to an editor every time, we built markdown, code, and diff viewers right into the terminal workspace.

posts.title: Knowing what your agents are doing without looking
posts.feature: Agent status detection / Sound notifications / Activity Dashboard
posts.summary: When you run 5 agents in parallel, you can't watch every terminal. We built status detection, sound notifications, and an activity dashboard so you don't have to.

posts.title: One sidebar, all your repos
posts.summary: When you work across 10+ repositories every day, you need a single place to see branches, worktrees, PRs, and diff stats — without switching tools.

posts.title: From the couch to 5G: how we built remote access
posts.summary: We wanted to check on AI agents without sitting at the desk. After trying session logs, remote proxies, and SSH, we ended up building a full mobile experience.

posts.title: Why we built Smart Prompts
posts.summary: We were copy-pasting the same instructions to AI agents every day. So we turned them into one-click actions that know your context.

posts.title: A terminal built for 8-hour sessions
posts.feature: Split panes / Detach to window / Persistent sessions / Find / Tab management
posts.summary: Split panes, detachable windows, persistent sessions, find in content, and up to 50 concurrent terminals. We built a terminal for people who live in it all day.

posts.title: Tracking your AI usage before the rate limit hits
posts.feature: Claude Usage Dashboard / Rate limits / Token tracking
posts.summary: Running multiple AI agents burns through rate limits fast. We built a usage dashboard so you can see exactly where you stand — and plan your work accordingly.

posts.title: How we keep it fast with 10 repos and 8 terminals open
posts.feature: Async git / PTY coalescing / Watcher cache / Bundle splitting
posts.summary: A desktop app that manages 10 repos, 8 terminals, and constant git operations can't afford to be slow. Here's the performance work we did under the hood.

posts.title: Talking to your AI agents instead of typing
posts.summary: Hold a key, speak your instruction, release. The text appears in the terminal. No cloud service, no API key, no latency — Whisper runs locally on your machine.

posts.title: Extend TUICommander with plugins
posts.feature: Plugin system / MCP / Sidebar panels / Context menus
posts.summary: Every workflow is different. We built a plugin system so you can extend TUICommander with custom panels, context menu actions, and MCP integrations.
